coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots doped tungstic anhydride on graphene oxide nanopanels: A new picomolar-range creatinine selective enzymeless electrochemical sensor
coredata.dc:creator: Ponnaiah, Sathish Kumar
Prakash, Periakaruppan
coredata.dc:description: The measurement of renal function by important clinical parameters such as (Crt) clearance and glomerular filtration rate often goes wrong vis-à-vis the Crt level in human body. Hence, development of an accurate detection system over a wide range of Crt concentration in both blood and urine is medically essential. In this study, a new non-enzymatic electrochemical probe, carbon dots doped tungstic anhydride embedded on graphene oxide nanopanels (CDs/WO3@GO) is reported for picomolar-level Crt detection in blood and urine with a wide linear range (0.2–112.0 nM). The sensor is economical, reproducible, stable and interferents-free. The properties of CDs/WO3@GO were studied using various analytical techniques. The proposed electrochemical Crt sensor could be used as a sustainable alternative for diagnostic use.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanocomposite
Modified electrode
CDs/WO3@GO
Creatinine
Urine and blood samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) UV–Vis spectra, (B) FT-IR spectra, (C) XRD patterns of CDs (curve a), CDs/WO3 (curve b), CDs/WO3@GO nanocomposite (curve c) (XRD inset is the enlarged view of the diffraction peaks in the 2θ range of 10.6–11.2).
FE-SEM images (a-c) with different magnifications of CDs/WO3@GO.
HR-TEM images of (a) CDs, (b) CDs/GO, (c & d) magnified images of CDs/WO3@GO, (e) SAED pattern of CDs/WO3@GO and (f) EDX image of CDs/WO3@GO.

(a) Nyquist plots for CDs, CDs/WO3 and CDs/WO3@GO obtained from EIS (inset: graph of expansion in high frequency region) and (b) CV response of bare GCE, CDs, WO3, GO, CDs/WO3, WO3@GO and CDs/WO3@GO modified GCE in the presence of Crt. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) CV curves with different scan rates (50–600 mV/s) for CDs/WO3@GO modified GCE (Crt concentration: 10 μM), (b) plot showing the linear dependence of oxidation peak current density vs square root of scan rate, (c) effect of various amount of CDs/WO3@GO on the peak current density and (d) relation between pH and peak current density.

(a) DPV response of CDs/WO3@GO modified GCE in 0.05 M PB (pH = 7.0), [Crt]: 0.2–112.0 nM, (b) plot of oxidation peak current density response vs. Crt concentration, (c) DPV response of CDs/WO3@GO/GCE to successive addition of the common interferences in the presence of 50 nM Crt and (d) CV cycles (30 nos.) with the addition of 50 μM Crt.
Application of CDs/WO3@GO nanocomposite for Crt detection.
Determination of Crt using various electrode materials.
Determination of blood and urine Crt using CDs/WO3@GO modified electrode.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Related standard deviation (RSD) of three independent experiments

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Value in parenthesis indicates concentration of Crt in original blood sample (undiluted) as obtained by multiplying with a dilution factor.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots doped tungstic anhydride on graphene oxide nanopanels: A new picomolar-range creatinine selective enzymeless electrochemical sensor

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Thiagarajar College, Madurai 625 009, Tamil Nadu, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Thiagarajar College, Madurai – 625 009, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The measurement of renal function by important clinical parameters such as (Crt) clearance and glomerular filtration rate often goes wrong vis-à-vis the Crt level in human body. Hence, development of an accurate detection system over a wide range of Crt concentration in both blood and urine is medically essential. In this study, a new non-enzymatic electrochemical probe, carbon dots doped tungstic anhydride embedded on graphene oxide nanopanels (CDs/WO3@GO) is reported for picomolar-level Crt detection in blood and urine with a wide linear range (0.2–112.0 nM). The sensor is economical, reproducible, stable and interferents-free. The properties of CDs/WO3@GO were studied using various analytical techniques. The proposed electrochemical Crt sensor could be used as a sustainable alternative for diagnostic use.

ce:section.ce:para: Creatine metabolism results in the production of creatinine (Crt, 2-amino-1-methyl-5H-imidazol-4-one) in the body, which is filtered constantly by kidneys [1,2]. When the Crt level is abnormal, the kidneys fail to function causing renal damage [3,4]. In adults, normal human fluid Crt levels are 53–106 μM (0.6–1.2 mg dL−1) in blood serum and 2.5–23 mM (37–300 mg dL−1) in urine [5–8]. The daily production of Crt is about 2% of the total body creatine. The functions of muscular, renal and thyroid are reflected in the level of Crt in human body [9–11]. A low level of Crt leads to hyperthyroidism, anemia, inflammatory muscle disease, extreme weight loss, advanced renal disease, renal stenosis, etc. A high level of Crt results in diabetes mellitus and hypothyroidism, chronic kidney disease and increased consumption of protein [12–20]. Therefore the accurate measurement of human blood and urine Crt is clinically very essential [7–15].

Till now, several analytical methods have been developed for the determination of Crt in biological fluids, which include capillary electrophoresis, chromatography, surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy, Jaffe reaction method, spectrophotometry, fluorimetry, enzymatic method, and chemiluminescence spectroscopy [21,23–32]. However, these methods have some negatives such as the requirement of (i) complicated and environmentally hazardous chemicals, (ii) arduous sample preparations, (iii) sophisticated and costly instruments, and (iv) well trained professionals for instruments operation [22,31]. Electrochemical methods have thus become prominent in relation to diagnostic testing of Crt clinically. Yet here also, the electrochemical methods which use bare/unmodified electrodes exhibit poor practicality and sensitivity with less electrochemical activity [33]. Therefore attention is being continuously focused on the improvement of the functions of the electrodes by modifying the electrodes in all possible ways [34–37].

Recently, electrodes modified with carbon dots (CDs) have been used in various electrochemical studies with good electro catalytic applications [38–40]. But the immobilization of CDs on the electrode surface is difficult, which limits their practical applications in electrochemistry [38]. Therefore it is imperative to incorporate other materials, especially meta-oxide semiconductor materials on CDs which prevents agglomeration and promote more catalytic active sites and sensing properties [41,42]. Among the various metal-oxides, tungstic anhydride (WO3) is found to be chemically stable and environmentally friendly which finds applications in batteries, solar cells, field emission devices, hydrogen production by water splitting, photo catalysis and sensors [43–45]. Moreover, WO3 is a promising low cost electro- catalyst, which has been used largely in electro analytical chemistry of various biological compounds due to its crystal phase structure, significant charge transfer processes, oxygen vacancy and surface state [46–50]. Again, the binary materials-combination is not able to meet the important provisos of sensing devices, which include selectivity, sensitivity and reproducibility [33]. Such problems are overcome by attaching graphene materials which provides unique electronic and optical properties with structural diversity [43,51–53]. The fact that graphene materials are ultimate electro active materials is further explained in electronic supporting material (ESM).

This study has utilized synergistically all the inherent special features of these three materials, namely CDs, WO3 and graphene oxide (GO). The novel ternary nanocomposite, CDs doped WO3 embedded on GO nanopanels (CDs/WO3@GO), has been synthesized by a one-pot method. The as-prepared nanocomposite was characterized by various analytical techniques such as ultraviolet -visible (UV–Vis) and Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy techniques, field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM), elemental mapping analysis, high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM), energy-dispersive X-ray analysis (EDX), selected area electron diffraction (SAED), X-ray diffraction (XRD), and thermogravimetric analysis (TGA). This novel electrochemical sensor made of the composite provides a wide detection range and lower detection limit and detects Crt level in human blood and urine samples, which is inexpensive, reproducible, and stable and demonstrates a good electro catalytic activity and selectivity towards Crt detection in the presence of various other similar biological entities. The elecrocatalytic behavior of this sensor is comparable or even superior compared to other existing equivalents.

Citric acid (CA), dimethyl formamide, tungsten hexachloride and other chemicals were procured from Sigma-Aldrich Company and used as received. NaH2PO4 and Na2HPO4 were used for preparing phosphate buffer (PB, 0.05 M). The pH adjustment was made using HCl and NaOH.

GO was prepared from graphite powder [52]. The nanocomposite composed of CDs, WO3 and GO was synthesized by a facile one-pot hydrothermal method. In a typical method, 0.35 g of WCl6 was mixed and ultrasonicated with dimethyl formamide solvent (15 mL), to which were added 1.673 g of CA and 0.1 g of GO slowly with constant stirring magnetically for 30 min. The optimal reaction ratio among CDs, WO3 and GO is calculated to be 1.0:0.21:0.06 respectively. The mixture was then kept at 100 °C for 2 h in an autoclave. The product obtained (CDs/WO3@GO) was cooled, washed with water and ethanol. Simultaneously, the binary composite CDs/WO3 was synthesized by the same procedure without adding GO. CDs were also prepared from CA separately [38].

The glassy carbon electrode (GCE) was first polished using aluminium oxide powder. It was then cleaned ultrasonically using water and ethanol. The dispersion of the nanocomposite, CDs/WO3@GO was obtained by dispersing it in 8 mL of water (1.0 mg mL−1), which was followed by ultrasonication for 10 min at room temperature. About 6 μL of ultrasonically dispersed CDs/WO3@GO in water was drop-casted on GCE. The GCE modified with the nanocomposite (CDs/WO3@GO/GCE) was dried in an air oven. Concurrently, the electrode modification was done with CDs, WO3, GO, CDs/WO3, WO3@GO by the same procedure. The electrochemical sensing studies were performed using these modified electrodes at room temperature. The synthetic procedure for CDs/WO3@GO is depicted in Scheme 1 . Cyclic voltammetry (CV) measurements were performed with a potential window ranging from −0.6 to 0.6 V at 50 mV/s. The potential window for differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) tests was fixed in the range from 0.1 to 0.5 V with amplitude of 0.05 V, and pulse width of 0.5 s.

JASCO (V-560) and FT-IR 460 Plus spectrophotometers were used for UV–Vis and FT-IR spectroscopic studies respectively. Characterization by XRD used X-ray diffracto meter (JEOL JDX 8030). The morphology and surface mapping analysis were investigated by FE-SEM, VEGA3 TESCAN. USA. TEM analysis used TEM, FEI TECNAI T20 G2 model. The electrochemical studies were performed on CH Instruments, 660E, Austin, TX Model with a routine three-electrode system comprised of bare GCE and modified electrodes as working electrodes and Pt wire and Ag/AgCl with internal KCl as auxiliary electrode and reference electrode respectively, at pH 7 (0.05 M PB) with a scan rate of 50 mV/s.

All the components were characterized and the formation of the nanocomposite, CDs/WO3@GO was confirmed firstly by UV–Vis spectroscopic analysis. The absorption spectra are shown in Fig. 1A, in which the curve a represents the spectrum of CDs. Here two peaks are observed at 222 and 315 nm, which correspond to π–π* transition of CC bonds and n-π* transitions of carbon cores respectively [54,55]. In curve b for CDs doped WO3, a band observed at 345 nm, corresponding to the absorption due to W atoms, indicates the existence of CDs/WO3 [55]. A predominant absorption peak at 335 nm with a weak peak at 215 nm is observed for CDs/WO3@GO (curve c) which confirms the successful assembly of the composite with maintaining of n-π* and π-π* transitions respectively of CO and carbon cores [56]. Thus it is inferred that the assemblage among CDs, WO3 and GO might have taken place via either of electrostatic binding or charge-transfer interactions [52].

The information regarding compositions and chemical functionalities was ascertained by FT-IR spectroscopic study. Fig. 1B shows the FT-IR spectra of CDs (curve a), CDs/WO3 (curve b) and CDs/WO3@GO (curve c). In curve a, there are three peaks observed at 3456, 1650 and 1115 cm−1 corresponding to OH, CO and C-O-C respectively [57]. Curve b for CDs/WO3 indicates one band in the frequency range of 780–795 cm−1 due to stretching vibrations of WO bond and the other at 515 cm−1 denoting bridging oxygen of O-W-O [58]. In the FT-IR spectrum of CDs/WO3@GO (curve c), there is a slight shift of peaks to lower wave numbers observed. The stretching vibrations of WO indicate a peak at 705–713 cm−1, epoxy stretching vibrations of C-O-C of phenolic hydroxyl group at 1022 cm−1, and C-OH and CO stretching vibrations of carbonyl derivatives at 1217 and 1714 cm−1. The bands at 3388 and 1590 cm−1 denote the stretching and bending vibrations of –OH in GO [59]. From these results, it is confirmed that the nanocomposite is very much composed of CDs, WO3 and GO.

The fine assembly of nanocomposite and its crystallinity was confirmed by XRD analysis. XRD patterns for CDs, CDs/WO3 and CDs/WO3@GO are shown in Fig. 1C. In curve a, which denotes the XRD pattern for CDs, a peak centered at 23.5° corresponding to (0 0 2) plane of carbon turbostratic phase (JCPDS card no. 79-1469) is observed [38]. Curve b for CDs/WO3 shows some sharp peaks appearing at around 14.9°, 23.2°, 27.1°, 28.1°, 33.9°, 47.4°, 58.2° and 78.4°, which can be indexed to the crystal planes of (1 0 0), (0 0 2), (1 0 2), (2 0 0), (1 1 2), (0 0 4), (4 0 0) and (4 0 4) respectively of WO3 hexagonal phase (JCPDS card no. 85-2460). An additional peak at 22.8° confirms the presence of CDs [60]. The high crystallinity of the nanostructure is revealed from the appearance of sharp and intense peaks. Similar to XRD pattern of CDs/WO3, a distinct peak at 10.85° is observed, as is seen in curve c, indicating the π-conjugated system characteristic of GO [52]. Thus XRD results prove the successful assemblage of the nanocomposite consisting of CDs, WO3, and GO and its crystalline nature.

FE-SEM analysis was carried out to study the properties such as size and surface morphology of the nanocomposite. The SEM images with different magnifications are shown in Fig. 2 (a-c). The images clearly reveal the wrinkled and scrolled intrinsic sheet like morphology for GO panels and distribution of CDs/WO3, thus proving the interconnection between the components. Elemental mapping analysis (Fig. 3 (a-d)) was also done on the nanocomposite, which shows that it consists of C, O and W. Hence, these results demonstrate the successful formation of crystalline CDs/WO3@GO [52]. To gain further understanding about the morphology of the as-synthesized CDs/WO3@GO, it was subjected to HR-TEM study. The HR-TEM images are shown in Fig. 4 (a-d). The HR-TEM image of CDs (Fig. 4a) indicates that the CDs are minute uniform spheres. The diameter of CDs is 6 nm approximately and the lattice spacing of the crystal is 0.21 nm, corresponding to (0 0 2) lattice plane of carbon turbostratic phase [61]. Fig. 4b clearly demonstrates that the black color CDs are engrained over GO nanostructure. The assembly of CDs/WO3 on the surface of GO is evidenced from Fig. 4c wherein the characteristic nanopanels with a unique three-dimensional GO network is seen [62]. The sizes of WO3 and CDs are found to be less than 26 nm and 6 nm, as exposed in Fig. 4d. The SAED pattern of CDs/WO3@GO is shown in Fig. 4e, which displays that the individual spots are associated with concentric rings indicating that the nanocomposite is made of many WO3 nanocrystals [63]. The EDX profile of CDs/WO3@GO, as shown in Fig. 4f, also confirms that the constituents, CDs and WO3 are embedded onto the surface of GO and the nanocomposite mainly consists of C, O and W [64]. The characterization of the materials was also done using TGA and Raman analyses and the respective texts are given in the ESM.

Electrochemical impedance spectra for CDs (black line), CDs/WO3 (blue line) and CDs/WO3@GO (red line) are shown in Fig. 5a. The charge transfer resistance at the electrode/electrolyte interface indicates the catalytic activity of the counter electrodes which could be obtained from the Nyquist plots of impedance spectra results. It is reported that smaller size of the semicircle arc diameter indicates a more effective separation of the photo generated pair or a faster interfacial charge transfer to the electron donor/acceptor. Compared to CDs/GCE and CDs/WO3/GCE, the CDs/WO3@GO/GCE demonstrates an obviously reduced semicircle at medium frequencies because GO greatly enhances the reaction kinetics of the electrode [65]. The CVs of unmodified GCE and GCE modified with CDs, WO3, GO, CDs/WO3, WO3@GO and CDs/WO3@GO with 10 μM Crt addition are shown in Fig. 5b (in 0.05 M PB, pH 7.0 and potential range from −0.6 to 0.6 V at 50 mV/s). It is observed from this Fig. 5b that the CDs/WO3@GO/GCE, upon addition of Crt, shows maximum affinity towards the electro catalytic oxidation of Crt with cathodic peak current density of about 29.5 μA compared to anodic peak current density of −14.6 μA. These results show that the electrode modification with CDs/WO3@GO enhances the redox ability towards Crt sensing [66–71]. The relationship between oxidation peak current (of 10 μM Crt) and scan rates (50–600 mV/s at pH 7.0) is shown in Fig. 6a. From the Fig. 6a, it can be seen that the redox current of Crt increases and the peaks potentials are shifted to positive and negative directions when the scan rates are increased, which infers that the quasi-reversible electron transfer takes place. The plot of redox behavior shows good linearity (Fig. 6b) with correlation coefficient (R2 = 0.9997), which implies that the electro oxidation process is diffusion controlled [66]. Electrochemical behavior of Crt and the respective texts are given in the ESM.

In order to realize the maximum efficiency of the GCE modified with CDs/WO3@GO for Crt assay, parameters such as catalyst load and pH were optimized [31,65]. The catalyst load was increased on the surface of the GCE from 2.0 to 10 μL and maximum sensitivity of the modified electrode was achieved (Fig. 6c) when 6.0 μL of CDs/WO3@GO (0.7 mg mL−1) was drop-casted. The increase of catalyst load increases the number of transport of electrons channels, rate of movement and the current response. When the amount of CDs/WO3@GO was further increased, the sensitivity decreases due to thickening of the material layer which hinders the transfer of electrons. 6.0 μL of CDs/WO3@GO (0.7 mg mL−1) on GCE surface is the optimum amount which balances the two factors. Also, the effect of pH was studied on the sensing of Crt. The cathodic peak current density response of Crt in different pH is shown in Fig. 6d. When the pH of the reaction solution is increased from 3.0 to 11.0, maximum cathodic current response of Crt is achieved at pH 7.0. Further increase of pH reduces current response. In pH lower than 7.0, the composite is protonated and the amine group of the Crt is not ionized. Hence, the adsorptive ability of Crt is decreased resulting in high over potential. On the other hand, in alkaline condition, the Crt becomes unstable and is easily oxidized, thus reducing the availability of target analyte molecules for sensing [31]. Thus pH 7.0 was maintained as optimum pH for efficient quantification of Crt.

Compared to conventional CV, DPV is more sensitive and has lower background current density. Also, the oxidation peak current can be detected evidently even at lower analyte concentrations. Therefore DPV study was performed to determine Crt under optimized experimental conditions using the proposed sensor system (CDs/WO3@GO/GCE). The current response values were obtained for various Crt concentrations (0.2 nM – 112.0 nM) in 0.05 M PB, pH 7.0 and potential range from 0 to 0.6 V. With the increase of Crt concentration, the peak current density at 0.32 V increases linearly as shown in Fig. 7 (a & b). This result infers that the electrode modified with CDs/WO3@GO can well be used as a prominent electrode material for Crt sensing [31]. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated using the relation:
(2)
LOD = 3σ / S
where σ is the standard deviation (0.47456 × 10−9) of the peak current for blank (n = 10) and S is the slope (11.864) of the first linear equation of Crt [38]. The LOD for the present sensor system is found to be 220 pM), with the correlation coefficient of 0.9896 and the sensitivity of the CDs/WO3@GO/GCE is estimated to be 1.5325 μA μM−1 cm−2. Table 1 compares the performance of various modified electrodes with the present CDs/WO3@GO/GCE in the determination of Crt. It is evident from the table that the CDs/WO3@GO/GCE electrochemical sensor developed herein provides a larger detection range and lower detection limit [10,15,18,22,32,67,69–78].

Two important factors that govern the selectivity of a sensor towards the analyte under study are functional groups of the related molecules and their size. Generally molecules with similar functional groups and size are more likely to interfere with the recognition of the target analyte molecules. Therefore, the system was evaluated by common interferences at physiological concentrations of glucose (3 mM), fructose (6 mM), ascorbic acid (62.5 μM), urea (3 mM), uric acid (62.5 μM), in the presence of 50 nM Crt (middle point level of linear range), followed by the addition of dopamine, human serum albumin, and L-cysteine, which was 20-fold higher than the concentration of Crt. DPV response was compared before and after the addition of these interferents. The results are shown in Fig. 7c, which indicate that the sensing system is interferents-free and very selective to Crt alone. A favorable electro catalytic environment is provided by a robust synergistic combination effect prompted between CDs/WO3@GO/GCE and Crt in which the aromatic Crt molecules easily approach to the electrode surface via electrostatic attraction as demonstrated in Scheme 2 . The connection between GO and CDs is mainly attributed to electrostatic adsorption, hydrogen bond or intermolecular force, originating from surface groups (-COOH, -NH2, -OH, etc.) from CDs and GO. The π–π stacking interaction between CDs/WO3@GO and modified electrode surface may accelerate the electron transfer, and weaken the other biological interferences on this modified electrode system [68,73]. Therefore, it is concluded from these results that the nanocomposite electrode, CDs/WO3@GO/GCE can be best used as an electrochemical sensor for the detection of Crt. A series of six repetitive experiments were carried out to assess the reproducibility of the sensor by comparing the DPV responses. The relative standard deviation (RSD) value is 1.14% with 80 μM Crt concentration, which indicates that the electrode modified with the nanocomposite is reproducible. The stability of the sensor was tested by preserving the modified electrode in a fridge at 4 °C for 4 weeks. After the 4 weeks period of storage, the current response was measured. The decrease of current density response is just 2.3% compared to the initial current (Fig. 7d). These results show that this sensor is highly stable which can be used for Crt detection for a longer time.

The real sample analysis on biological fluids for clinical diagnosis is very important. The practical feasibility of the proposed sensor was studied by involving the CDs/WO3@GO/GCE in the quantification of Crt in human blood and urine samples (Table 2 ). The Crt content in two different age groups of urine and blood samples were obtained from healthy volunteers and were filtered before analysis. In order to evaluate the interferences and the matrix effects, samples were diluted at 1:100 and 1:150 ratios respectively for blood and urine samples with the working buffers and centrifuged for 10 min at 6000 rpm and the supernatant was stored at −4 °C until used for Crt determination. Furthermore, the proposed sensor and the commercial ELISA Kit were used for the determination of Crt concentration in urine and blood samples as shown in Table S1, and their detection results are in good agreement. The precision of the detection of Crt using standard addition method was ascertained by DPV. Satisfactory recoveries are able to be obtained, which clearly proves the consistency of the proposed sensor for real time monitoring of samples.

The present work demonstrates a novel strategy to prepare CDs/WO3@GO nanocomposite by a facile one-pot hydrothermal method. The GCE modified with the synthesized nanocomposite shows an excellent electro catalytic behavior towards Crt. The detection system is not affected by related molecules with similar functional groups and size and it is very selective. Under the optimized conditions, the current signals are found to correlate linearly with the Crt concentration in the range of 0.2–112.0 nM (R2 = 0.9896) with a detection limit of 220 pM. The sensor exhibits excellent reproducibility and stability. The elecrocatalytic behavior of this sensor is comparable or even superior performance of Crt-sensing is exhibited by CDs/WO3@GO compared to other analogous sensors. The practicality of CDs/WO3@GO composite nanomaterial may facilitate the application of such a new platform for detecting Crt in clinical diagnosis.

ce:para.ce:sans-serif: Sathish Kumar Ponnaiah:Investigation, Methodology, Writing - original draft.Prakash Periakaruppan:Conceptualization, Supervision, Data curation, Methodology, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no competing financial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The “Catalyzed and Financial support” by Tamilnadu State Council for Science and Technology, Department of Higher Education, Government of Tamilnadu, India for the award of research funding for research scholars (RFRS) scheme (TNSCST/RFRS/VR/07/2018-19) is very much acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.msec.2020.111010.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A dual signal on-off fluorescent nanosensor for the simultaneous detection of copper and creatinine

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R.V. Nair, P.R.P. Suma, R.S. Jayasree, A dual signal on-off fluorescent nanosensor for the simultaneous detection of copper and creatinine, Mater. Sci. Eng., C, 109 (2020) 110569-110601.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Bioenzymatic creatine biosensor based on reflectance measurement for real-time monitoring of fish freshness

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F.F. Fazial, L.L. Tan, S.I. Zubairi, Bioenzymatic creatine biosensor based on reflectance measurement for real-time monitoring of fish freshness, Sens. Actuators B: Chem., 269 (2018) 36-45.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Creatinine: from physiology to clinical application

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Kashani, M.H. Rosner, M. Ostermann, Creatinine: From physiology to clinical application, Eur. J. Int. Med. 72 (2020) 9-14.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Spectrophotometric assay of creatinine in human serum sample

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G.J. Kalaivani and S.K. Suja, Enzyme-less sensing of kidney dysfunction biomarker - creatinine using inulin based bio-nanocomposite, New J. Chem., 43 (2019) 5914-5924

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A calorimetric biosensing system for quantification of urinary creatinine

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Gaddes, W. B. Reeves, and S. Tadigadapa, A Calorimetric biosensing system for quantification of urinary creatinine, ACS Sens. 2(6) (2017) 796-802.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: AIEE active supramolecular metallic ensemble as “turn-on” platform for detection of creatinine in human blood serum at picomolar level

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Kaur, M. Kataria, M. Kaur, A. K. Sood, M. Kumar, and V. Bhalla, AIEE active supramolecular metallic ensemble as “turn-on” platform for detection of creatinine in human blood serum at picomolar level, ACS Sustainable Chem. Eng. 7 (2019) 14829−14833.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Green synthesis of gluten-stabilized fluorescent gold quantum clusters: application as turn-on sensing of human blood creatinine

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.S. Mathew and K. Joseph, Green synthesis of gluten-stabilized fluorescent gold quantum clusters: application as turn-on sensing of human blood creatinine, ACS Sustainable Chem. Eng. 5 (2017) 4837−4845.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Novel amperometric flow-injection analysis of creatinine using a molecularly-imprinted polymer coated copper oxide nanoparticle modified carbon-paste-electrode

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N. Nontawong, M. Amatatongchai, S. Thimoonnee, S. Laosing, P. Jarujamrus, C. Karuwan, S. Anal. 175 (2019) 112770-112808.
